items.title: Fibrocystic mastopathy: definition
items.contentHtml:
Definition of fibrocystic mastopathy

  • Fibrocystic mastopathy is a benign condition of the breast tissue characterized by proliferative and regressive changes of the stroma and ductal/lobular epithelium, mediated by an abnormal hormonal response to estrogens and progesterone.
  • Histologically it includes stromal fibrosis, cyst formation (due to ductal dilation and acinar), adenosis, epithelial hyperplasia and apocrine metaplasia.
  • It is not a single disease but rather a spectrum of benign histological findings, and its presence does not imply a high risk of cancer per se, unless there is atypical hyperplasia.

items.title: Clinic: cyclical pain and nodules
items.contentHtml:
Clinic: cyclic pain and nodules

  • Cyclic breast pain (cyclic mastalgia) typically occurs in the late luteal phase, bilateral and diffuse, with intensity that is relieved after menstruation; It is the most common symptom in fibrocystic mastopathy.
  • The nodules are multiple, bilateral, with poorly defined borders and a rubbery or nodular consistency, which increase in size and sensitivity premenstrually.
  • The cyclical symptoms are key data for the differential diagnosis with cancer, where the pain is usually constant and located .

items.title: Fibroadenoma: most common solid tumor
items.contentHtml:
Fibroadenoma: most common solid tumor

  • Fibroadenoma is the most common benign solid tumor of the breast, with maximum incidence between 20 and 35 years; It is of mixed origin (stromal and epithelial) and is hormonally dependent.
  • Clinically it presents as a single, mobile nodule, with clear edges, elastic consistency and is not painful, although they can be multiple in 10-20% of cases.
  • Histologically it is classified into simple fibroadenoma (low risk of malignancy) and fibroadenoma. complex (with cysts, sclerosing adenosis, calcifications or epithelial hyperplasia, conferring a slightly increased risk of cancer).

items.title: Ultrasound as a first-line study
items.contentHtml:
Ultrasound as a first-line study

  • In women under 40 years of age with a palpable nodule, breast ultrasound is the first-line imaging study, as it allows differentiation of cystic lesions from solid lesions without ionizing radiation.
  • In women over 40 years of age, mammography is preferred as the first study, complemented by ultrasound if there is a nodule. palpable or high breast density.
  • Ultrasound evaluates benign criteria: oval shape, circumscribed edges, orientation parallel to the skin, homogeneous echostructure and posterior acoustic reinforcement; If all are met, it is classified as BI-RADS 2 (benign) or 3 (probably benign).

items.title: Mandatory triple diagnostic evaluation
items.contentHtml:
Mandatory triple diagnostic evaluation

  • The triple diagnostic evaluation is the gold standard for any palpable breast nodule or suspicious imaging finding: it combines clinical examination, imaging (mammography and/or ultrasound) and percutaneous biopsy (core needle puncture or biopsy assisted by empty).
  • If the three components are concordant and benign, imaging follow-up can be performed; if there is discordance, the biopsy should be repeated or surgical excision should be performed.
  • Core needle biopsy is the method of choice for solid lesions, as it provides sufficient tissue for histological diagnosis and evaluation of atypia.

items.title: Ultrasound signs of benignity (BI-RADS 2-3)
items.contentHtml:
Ultrasound signs of benignity (BI-RADS 2-3)

  • Ultrasound signs of a typical benign lesion (classifiable as BI-RADS 2 or BI-RADS 3) include: oval or rounded shape, circumscribed margins (smooth and well-defined), orientation parallel to the plane of the skin (wider than high), homogeneous echostructure (isoechogenic or hyperechogenic with respect to adipose tissue) and posterior acoustic reinforcement.
  • The absence of posterior acoustic shadowing and the presence of a thin echogenic halo also support benignity.
  • A lesion with these characteristics can be managed with ultrasound follow-up at 6 months (BI-RADS 3) or considered benign (BI-RADS 2).

items.title: Core needle biopsy if suspected
items.contentHtml:
Core needle biopsy if suspected

  • Core needle biopsy is indicated for any solid nodule with suspicious ultrasound features (BI-RADS 4A or higher) or when the triple diagnostic evaluation is not concordantly benign.
  • It provides cylinders of tissue 1-2 mm in diameter that They allow complete histological diagnosis, including evaluation of atypia and differentiation between in situ and invasive carcinoma.
  • Fine needle aspiration biopsy (FNAB) is only indicated for simple cysts or to confirm lymph node metastasis; is not sufficient for the diagnosis of primary solid lesions.

items.title: Conservative treatment in people under 35
items.contentHtml:
Conservative treatment in people under 35

  • In women under 35 years of age with a typical fibroadenoma (confirmed by triple diagnostic evaluation concordant benign), the management of choice is conservative: clinical and ultrasound observation every 6-12 months.
  • The surgical indication is reserved for: documented growth (>20% increase in volume in 6 months), persistent pain that does not respond to analgesics, suspicion of atypia in biopsy, or patient's desire.
  • In people over 35 years of age, excision is considered if the nodule is >2 cm or there are additional risk factors (family history of breast cancer).

items.title: Cyclic mastalgia: management with NSAIDs
items.contentHtml:
Cyclic mastalgia: management with NSAIDs

  • The first-line management of cyclic mastalgia is topical or systemic non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen 400-600 mg every 8 hours during premenstrual days, with gastric protection if used regularly. chronic.
  • It is also recommended: use of an appropriate sports bra, reducing caffeine and saturated fats, and applying local cold compresses.
  • If the pain is intense and refractory, danazol (200-400 mg/day) or tamoxifen (10 mg/day) can be considered for short periods, but with significant adverse effects (hirsutism, voice changes, risk of thrombosis).

items.title: Galactography for pathological secretion
items.contentHtml:
Galactography for pathological secretion

  • galactography (or ductography) is indicated in patients with unilateral, spontaneous, non-milk pathological secretion, especially if it is bloody or serosanguinous, to identify intraductal papillomas or intraductal lesions.
  • It consists of cannulation of the secreting duct with a fine probe and injection of radiopaque contrast, followed by mammography in craniocaudal and lateral projections.
  • If an intraductal lesion is identified, surgical biopsy with harpoon localization or ductoscopy with directed biopsy is performed; the presence of atypia or ductal carcinoma in situ requires complete excision.

items.foco: Non-increased cancer risk in simple fibroadenoma
items.title: Non-increased cancer risk in simple fibroadenoma
items.contentHtml:
No increased risk of cancer in simple fibroadenoma

  • The simple fibroadenoma (without atypia, without histological complexity) does not confer an increased risk of breast cancer compared to the general population; therefore, it does not require imaging follow-up beyond that usual for the age.
  • In contrast, complex fibroadenoma (with cysts, sclerosing adenosis, calcifications or epithelial hyperplasia) is associated with a relative risk of 1.5-2.0 for breast cancer, especially if there is associated atypical hyperplasia.
  • The presence of atypia in the biopsy of a fibroadenoma requires complete surgical excision and closer imaging follow-up (annual mammography).

items.title: Surgical indication: growth or atypia
items.contentHtml:
Surgical indication: growth or atypia

  • The absolute indications for surgical excision of a benign breast nodule (fibroadenoma or fibrocystic mastopathy lesion) are: documented growth (>20% increase in volume in 6 months or diameter >3 cm), atypia on biopsy (atypical ductal or lobular hyperplasia), suspected malignancy in the triple diagnostic evaluation discordant, or disabling pain that does not respond to medical treatment.
  • Surgery is also considered if the nodule is >2 cm in women over 35 years of age or if the patient requests it due to anxiety or deformity aesthetic.
  • The technique of choice is tumorectomy with a margin of healthy tissue, and the specimen must be sent for complete histopathological study.
